---
title: "ABUNDANCE OF EASTERN NORTH PACIFIC GRAY WHALES 2025/2026"
author: "Tomo Eguchi, Aimee Lang, David Weller"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: 
  bookdown::word_document2: default
  header-includes:
  - \usepackage{ragged2e}
  - \renewcommand{\footnotesize}{\10 \justify}
bibliography: reference.bib
csl: marine-ecology-progress-series.csl  
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
rm(list = ls())
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
save.fig <- T
min.dur <- 60
WinBUGS.iter <- 100000
WinBUGS.RunDate <- "2026-02-20"

source("Granite_Canyon_Counts_fcns.R")
library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(flextable)
library(readr)
library(segmented)
library(knitr)

# Define the analysis year.
YEAR <- 2026

# File names for bringing in data and results:
N.hats.new.filename <- paste0("Data/WinBUGS_abundance_2007to2026_v2_min", min.dur,
                              "_", format(WinBUGS.iter, scientific = F, big.mark = ""), 
                              "_", WinBUGS.RunDate, ".csv")
#data.new.filename <- paste0("RData/V2.1_Feb2026/out_2026_min", min.dur, "_Tomo_v2.rds")
WinBUGS.results.filename <- paste0("RData/WinBUGS_2007to2026_v2_min", min.dur, "_", 
                                   format(WinBUGS.iter, scientific = F, big.mark = ""),
                                   "_", WinBUGS.RunDate, ".rds")

# knit_hooks$set(inline = function(x) {
#   prettyNum(x, big.mark = ",")
# })

format.big.number <- function(x) {
  format(x, scientific=F, digits = 6, big.mark = ",")
}

set_flextable_defaults(font.size = 9,
                       font.family = "Cambria")

# Estimates from Laake et al. are here:
col.defs <- cols(Year = col_character(),
                 Nhat = col_double(),
                 CV = col_double())

# Function from Laake's code
conf.int = function(abundance, CV, alpha=0.05, digits=2, prt=FALSE){
  # Computes confidence intervals based on lognormal distr.
  # JMB / NMML / 11 Sep 2008
  
  if (alpha <0 || alpha > .999) stop("alpha must be in (0,1)")
  z = round(abs(qnorm(alpha/2)),2)
  if (prt) cat("N:",abundance,"  cv:",CV,"  alpha:",alpha,"  z:",z,"\n")
  C <- exp(z * sqrt(log(1 + CV^2)))
  SL <- round(abundance/C,digits)
  SU <- round(abundance * C,digits)
  data.frame(SL,SU)
}

Laake.estimates <- read_csv(file = "Data/Laake et al 2012 Table 9 Nhats.csv",
                            col_types = col.defs)

Laake.CI <- conf.int(abundance = Laake.estimates$Nhat, CV = Laake.estimates$CV)

Laake.estimates %>% 
  mutate(SE = CV * Nhat,
         LCL = Laake.CI$SL,
         UCL = Laake.CI$SU,
         Season = lapply(strsplit(Year, "_"), 
                         FUN = function(x) paste0(x[1], "/", x[2])) %>% 
           unlist) %>%
  dplyr::select(Season, Nhat, SE, LCL, UCL)  %>%
  mutate(Year = lapply(str_split(Season, "/"), 
                       FUN = function(x) x[2]) %>% 
           unlist() %>% 
           as.numeric()) -> Laake.estimates

# Bring in data from Rugh et al. 2001 (median dates)
Rugh.data <- read_csv(file = "Data/Rugh et al 2001 Table 1 Median Date.txt",
                      col_types = cols(Season = col_character(),
                                       Median_Date = col_date(format = "%Y-%m-%d"))) %>%
  mutate(Year = lapply(str_split(Season, "/"), 
                       FUN = function(x) x[2]) %>% 
           unlist() %>% 
           as.numeric(),
         median.idx = (Median_Date - as.Date(paste0((Year-1), "-12-01"))) %>%
           as.numeric())

# Durban estimates:
seasons <- c("2006/2007", "2007/2008", "2009/2010", "2010/2011", 
             "2014/2015", "2015/2016", "2019/2020", "2021/2022",
             "2022/2023", "2023/2024", "2024/2025", "2025/2026")

# The most recent estimates (2023/2024) are here:
N.hats.new <- read_csv(file = N.hats.new.filename,
                        col_types = cols(row.ID = col_character(),
                                         Season = col_character(),
                                         total.mean = col_double(),
                                         total.CV = col_double(),
                                         total.median = col_double(),
                                         total.LCL = col_double(),
                                         total.UCL = col_double())) %>%
  mutate(Year = lapply(str_split(Season, "/"), 
                       FUN = function(x) x[2]) %>% 
           unlist() %>% 
           as.numeric(),
         Method = "Durban",
         SE = total.mean * total.CV/100)

# From Durban et al. 2015, Fig 1 (J. Cetacean Res. Manage. 15:61-68)
Durban.2015 <- data.frame(Year = c(2007, 2008, 2010, 2011),
                          Season = seasons[1:4],
                          Nhat = c(20750, 17820, 21210, 20990),
                          LCL = c(18860, 16150, 19420, 19230),
                          UCL = c(23320, 19920, 23250, 22900),
                          SE = NA,
                          Method = "Durban")

# From Eguchi et al. 2023, Table 1 (NOAA Tech Memo NMFS-SWFSC-680)
# Eguchi.2023 <- data.frame(Year = c(2015, 2016, 2020, 2022, 2023),
#                           Season = seasons[5:9],
#                           Nhat = c(28790, 26960, 20580, 16650, 14526),
#                           LCL = c(23620, 24420, 18700, 15170, 13194.8),
#                           UCL = c(39210, 29830, 22870, 18335, 16040),
#                           Method = "Durban")

# From Eguchi et al. 2025, Table 1 (NOAA Tech Memo NMFS-SWFSC-724)
Eguchi.2025 <- data.frame(Year = c(2015, 2016, 2020, 2022, 2023, 2024, 2025),
                          Season = seasons[5:11],
                          Nhat = c(23585, 27430, 20110, 17470, 14770, 19730,12950),
                          LCL = c(21290.0, 24930.0, 18300.0, 15840.0, 13410.0, 17850.0, 11690.0),
                          UCL = c(26410.0, 30180.0, 22060.0, 19330.0, 16250.0, 21850.2, 14450.2),
                          SE = NA,
                          Method = "Durban")

# UMEs
UMEs <- data.frame(Season = c("1999/2000", "2000/2001", 
                              "2019/2020", "2020/2021", 
                              "2021/2022", "2022/2023"),
                   Year = c(1999, 2000, 2019, 2020, 2021, 2022))

# This is for the 2024/2025 analysis. 
end.year <- 2026
all.years <- data.frame(Year = seq(from = min(Laake.estimates$Year), 
                                   to = end.year))

# GEt the WinBUGS results
WinBUGS.results <- readRDS(WinBUGS.results.filename)
n.array <- WinBUGS.results$BUGS.input$data$n

# Observer list gets updated as new observers are added. 
x <- length(seasons)

# Uses raw data files to compute summary statistics for each year - OLD
# This process has been completed within Extract_Data_All_v2.Rmd. So,
# use the output from that script. 2026-02-24
summary.season <- function(season.name, min.dur){
  stats.season <- readRDS(file = paste0("RData/V2.1_Feb2026/out_", 
                                        season.name, "_min", min.dur, 
                                        "_Tomo_v2.rds"))
  stats.season$Season <- season.name
  return(stats.season)
}

years.to.summarize <- c(2015, 2016, 2020, 2022, 2023, 2024, 2025, 2026)

# 2025-02-28
# I found some errors in data files and they were edited. These were
# 2015-01-09: Added "B" at the beginning of the day
# 2019-12-19: Added "B" at 12:46:32
# 2023-02-06: Removed an extra "V" at the end of the day
yrs.summary <- lapply(years.to.summarize, FUN = summary.season, min.dur = min.dur)

new.summary <- yrs.summary[[length(years.to.summarize)]]

whale.groups.daily.list <- lapply(yrs.summary, 
                                  FUN = function(x) {
                                    x$Data_Out %>%
                                      dplyr::select(BeginDay, begin, end, dur, n, npods) %>%
                                      mutate(Season = x$Season,
                                             cumu.Effort = cumsum(dur),
                                             DOS = as.numeric(BeginDay),
                                             cumu.N = cumsum(n)) %>%
                                      rename(Effort = dur)
                                  })

whale.groups.daily.df <- do.call(rbind, whale.groups.daily.list) %>%
  mutate(Season.f = as.factor(Season))

p.1 <- ggplot(whale.groups.daily.df) +
  geom_point(aes(x = DOS, y = cumu.Effort, color = cumu.N)) +
  facet_wrap(~Season.f)

```

## Introduction {.unnumbered}

The Southwest Fisheries Science Center (SWFSC) consistently monitors the abundance of eastern North Pacific (ENP) gray whales (*Eschrichtius robustus*) through shore-based surveys. These visual surveys, conducted off central California between December and February during the whales' southward migration, contribute to a long-term time series of abundance estimates that began in 1967 (Laake et al. (2012)).

The abundance of ENP gray whales showed an increasing trend until the 2015/2016 season. However, subsequent estimates indicated a significant decline, dropping from 26,960 whales (95% CI = 24,420-29,830) in 2015/2016 to 14,526 whales (95% CI = 13,194 - 16,040) in 2022/2023 (Eguchi et al. 2023a). While the 2022/2023 estimate remained higher than the 1967 baseline (13,426 whales, CV = 0.094, Laake et al. 2012), this consecutive decline raised concerns about the population's status.

Data from the 2023/2024 season suggested an increase in abundance (Eguchi et al. 2024). This apparent increase, however, was deemed biologically unplausible over a two-year period, suggesting other factors were influencing the count. Compounding these concerns, the following season (2024/2025) saw another significant decline, with an estimated abundance of 12,950 whales (95% CI = 11,690-14,450, Eguchi et al. 2025), further alarming researchers about the population's health.

This report will present the new abundance estimate for ENP gray whales based on the data collected during the 2025/2026 southward migration season off the central California coast.

## Methods {.unnumbered}

Data for this updated abundance estimate were collected during the `r YEAR-1`/`r YEAR` southward ENP gray whale migration. Counts were made from a shore-based watch station at Granite Canyon, California, by observer pairs rotating from a larger pool. Each survey day was split into six 90-minute shifts. As in previous analyses since 2006, only shifts with at least 60 minutes of survey effort were included. Some shifts were less than 60 minutes due to inclement weather and less-than-ideal sighting conditions (e.g., Beaufort sea state greater than 4). Sampling and analytical methods are described in a previous publication (@durban2015a.

The estimated abundance reported here was computed using the N-mixture modeling approach used previously by SWFSC for surveys conducted between the 2006/2007 and `r seasons[length(seasons)-1]` seasons (Durban et al. 2015, 2017, Stewart and Weller 2021a, Eguchi et al. 2022a, 2023a, 2024). In this approach, the sighting probability of shore-based observers was estimated by using data from replicate surveys (i.e., data collected simultaneously by two independent observer teams) that were completed in the  2009/2010 and 2010/2011 seasons and covariates that affect sighting probabilities (i.e., visibility, sea state, and observers). These sighting probability estimates allowed the total number of whales passing through the survey area during a watch period to be estimated from the observed number of whales, even in years when replicate surveys were not conducted.

In the analysis, the start date of the southward migration for the Granite Canyon study site is set to 1 December, and the end date to 28 February, with the number of whales passing the watch station on those two dates assumed to be zero. The daily count data are assumed to be random deviates from binomial distributions with the estimated sighting probability and the true but unknown number of whales in the sampling area, which is assumed to change as a function of the number of days since 1 December. The model fits two possible functions to the daily whale counts and selects the one that best fits each count.  These functions are (i) a normal distribution with the peak in the daily number of whales passing occurring at the midpoint of the migration and (ii) a spline fit that allows the change in the daily number of whales to flexibly match the observed daily counts without expectations about the shape of the curve. The model then internally selects the candidate function that best matches the daily number of observed whales. The final abundance estimate is the sum of the estimated number of whales passing the survey area each day, with a correction factor applied to account for those that migrate through the study area at night (Perryman et al. 1999). The modeling approach is described in detail in Durban et al. (2015; 2017).

Because the N-mixture modeling approach uses all data since the 2006/2007 season to estimate parameters  shared across yearly datasets, estimated abundances change as more data are added to the analysis. Before the 2024 report (Eguchi et al. 2024), only the new estimate was provided, while estimates for previous years were retained as they had been in the reports. In the 2024 report, we provided both old and new estimates. In this report, we provide only updated estimates for all years since the 2006/2007 season. For old estimates, please see Eguchi et al. (2024). 

## Results and Discussion {.unnumbered}

```{r results, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message= FALSE}
#estimates.2023 <- read_csv(file = "Data/abundance_2023_85min.csv")

Laake.estimates %>%
  dplyr::select(Year, Season, Nhat, LCL, UCL, SE) %>%
  mutate(Method = "Laake") %>%
  rbind(Durban.2015) %>%
  rbind(Eguchi.2025) -> all.estimates.old

Laake.estimates %>%
  dplyr::select(Year, Season, Nhat, LCL, UCL, SE) %>%
  mutate(Method = "Laake") %>%
  rbind(N.hats.new %>% 
          dplyr::select(Year, Season, total.median, total.LCL, total.UCL, SE) %>%
          transmute(Year = Year,
                    Season = Season,
                    Nhat = total.median,
                    LCL = total.LCL,
                    UCL = total.UCL,
                    SE = SE,
                    Method = "Durban")) -> all.estimates.new

all.estimates.new$Nhat.2025 <- c(all.estimates.old$Nhat, NA)


#write.csv(all.estimates, file = "Data/all_estimates_2023.csv")
# Create all years without skipping unobserved years:
# all.years %>% mutate(Year0 = Year - 1,
#                      Season = paste(Year0, Year, sep = "/")) %>%
#   left_join(all.estimates.old) -> all.years.2023.df

all.years %>% mutate(Year0 = Year - 1,
                     Season = paste(Year0, Year, sep = "/")) %>%
  left_join(all.estimates.new) -> all.years.new.df

write.csv(all.years.new.df, 
          file = paste0("Data/Nhats_", YEAR, ".csv"))

p.Nhat.new <- ggplot(data = all.years.new.df) + 
  geom_point(aes(x = Season, y = Nhat, color = Method)) + 
  geom_errorbar(aes(x = Season, ymin = LCL, ymax = UCL, color = Method),
                size = 1) +
  geom_tile(data = UMEs,
            aes(x = Season, y = 22500, width = 1, height = 35000),
            fill = "gold", alpha = 0.3) +
  scale_color_discrete(breaks = c("Durban", "Laake")) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, size = 8),
        legend.position = "top") +
  xlab("Season") +
  ylab("Abundance")

if (save.fig)
  ggsave(filename = paste0("Figures/nhats_", YEAR, ".png"), 
         plot = p.Nhat.new,
         device = "png", dpi = 600)


# Find daily data:
new.summary$Complete_Data %>% 
  group_by(BeginDay) %>%
  summarize(Date = as.Date("2025-11-30") + BeginDay[1],
            n.day = sum(n),
            n.group = sum(npods),
            .groups = "drop") -> daily.counts

new.summary$Data_Out %>% 
  group_by(BeginDay) %>%
  summarize(Date = as.Date("2025-11-30") + BeginDay[1],
            n.day = sum(n),
            n.group = sum(npods),
            .groups = "drop") -> daily.counts_Data_Out

new.summary$Final_Data %>% 
  group_by(BeginDay) %>%
  summarize(Date = as.Date("2025-11-30") + BeginDay[1],
            n.day = sum(n),
            n.group = sum(npods),
            .groups = "drop") -> daily.counts_Final_Data

# Get the output from WinBUGS run. I use the version 2 output results
# because V1 had some errors for the past years.  

Nhat.new <- WinBUGS.results$BUGS.out$mean$Corrected.Est[x] 
SD.Nhat.new <- WinBUGS.results$BUGS.out$sd$Corrected.Est[x] 
CV.Nhat.new <- SD.Nhat.new/Nhat.new

# All data:
# Extract estimated counts
Daily.Est <- WinBUGS.results$BUGS.out$sims.list$Daily.Est
sp <- WinBUGS.results$BUGS.out$sims.list$sp
com <- WinBUGS.results$BUGS.out$sims.list$com
Corrected.Est <- WinBUGS.results$BUGS.out$sims.list$Corrected.Est

n <- WinBUGS.results$BUGS.input$data$n

stats.list <- vector(mode = "list",
                     length = dim(Daily.Est)[3])

for (k in 1:dim(Daily.Est)[3]){
  # Daily.Est.list[[k]] <- Daily.Est[,,k]
  # Daily.Est.UCIs[[k]] <- apply(Daily.Est[,,k],2,quantile,0.975)
  # Daily.Est.LCIs[[k]] <- apply(Daily.Est[,,k],2,quantile,0.275)
  # 
  # sp.list[[k]] <- sp[,,k]
  # com.list[[k]] <- com[,,k]
  
  stats.list[[k]] <- data.frame(Daily.Est.median = apply(Daily.Est[,,k], 2,
                                                            median),
                                   Daily.Est.LCL = apply(Daily.Est[,,k], 2,
                                                         quantile,0.275),
                                   Daily.Est.UCL = apply(Daily.Est[,,k], 2,
                                                         quantile,0.975),
                                   sp.median = apply(exp(sp[,,k]), 2,
                                                     median),
                                   sp.LCL = apply(exp(sp[,,k]), 2,
                                                  quantile,0.025),
                                   sp.UCL = apply(exp(sp[,,k]), 2,
                                                  quantile,0.975),
                                   com.median = apply(exp(com[,,k]), 2,
                                                      median),
                                   com.LCL = apply(exp(com[,,k]), 2,
                                                   quantile,0.025),
                                   com.UCL = apply(exp(com[,,k]), 2,
                                                   quantile,0.975),
                                   #total.median = apply(exp(sp[,,k]), 1, sum),
                                   days = 1:dim(Daily.Est)[2],
                                   Season = seasons[k])
}


all.stats <- do.call("rbind", stats.list) %>% group_by(Season)

estimate.new <- all.estimates.new %>% filter(Year == YEAR)
estimate.last.year <- all.estimates.new %>% filter(Year == (YEAR-1))


# find daily effort: there are 90 days, where 1st and 90th are zeros.
# This was already done above. yrs.summary - compare them with 
# the code here. 
daily.effort <- daily.n <- daily.N <- matrix(nrow = 88, ncol = x)
Watch.Length <- WinBUGS.results$BUGS.input$data$Watch.Length
day.mat <- WinBUGS.results$BUGS.input$data$day
ns <- rbind(n[,1,], matrix(NA, nrow = 2, ncol = x))

for (y in 1:x){
  for (j in 2:89){
    daily.effort[j-1, y] <- sum(Watch.Length[day.mat[1:nrow(day.mat) , y] == j, y], 
                                na.rm = T)
    daily.n[j-1, y] <- sum(ns[day.mat[1:nrow(day.mat) ,y] == j, y], na.rm = T)
    daily.N[j-1, y] <- daily.n[j-1,y]/daily.effort[j-1,y]
  }
}

daily.N.df <- data.frame(daily.N) 
colnames(daily.N.df) <- seasons
daily.N.df %>% pivot_longer(everything(),
                            names_to = "Season", 
                            values_to = "N") %>%  
  arrange(Season) -> daily.N.df.long

daily.N.df.long$days <- rep(2:89, times = x)

daily.n.df <- data.frame(daily.n) 
colnames(daily.n.df) <- seasons
daily.n.df %>% pivot_longer(everything(),
                            names_to = "Season", 
                            values_to = "n") %>%  
  arrange(Season) -> daily.n.df.long

daily.n.df.long$days <- rep(2:89, times = x)

p.daily <- 
  ggplot(data = all.stats) +
  geom_line(aes(x = days, y = Daily.Est.median)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(x = days, 
                  ymin = Daily.Est.LCL, 
                  ymax = Daily.Est.UCL),
              fill = "orange", 
              alpha = 0.5) + 
  # geom_point(data = daily.n.df.long,
  #            aes(x = days, y = n)) +
  geom_point(data = daily.N.df.long,
             aes(x = days, y = N)) +
  facet_wrap(vars(Season))+
  xlab("Days since December 1") + 
  ylab("Whales per day")

if (save.fig)
  ggsave(filename = paste0("Figures/daily_estimates_", YEAR, ".png"), 
         plot = p.daily,
         device = "png", dpi = 600)

# Using yrs.summary to see how many hours were spent on surveys as total (not 
# eliminating short shifts)
survey.hrs.new <- new.summary$Data_Out$dur * 24 

```

From `r format(as.Date(paste0(YEAR - 1, "-11-30")) + new.summary$Data_Out[1, "BeginDay"], "%d %B %Y")` to `r format(as.Date(paste0(YEAR - 1, "-11-30")) + new.summary$Data_Out[nrow(new.summary$Data_Out), "BeginDay"], "%d %B %Y")`, `r length(unique(new.summary$Data_Out$obs))` trained observers completed `r signif(sum(survey.hrs.new), 4)` hours of survey effort over `r nrow(daily.counts_Data_Out)` survey days. A total of `r sum(daily.counts_Data_Out$n.group)` groups of `r sum(daily.counts_Data_Out$n.day)` whales were counted. The highest daily count was `r max(daily.counts_Data_Out$n.day)` whales on `r daily.counts_Data_Out %>% filter(n.day == max(n.day)) %>% dplyr::select("Date") %>% as.data.frame() %>% format("%d %B %Y")` (Figure \@ref(fig:Figure-daily-fit)). Due to inclement weather and other constraints (e.g., poor visibility from fog and rain), some observation periods were \< 60 min and were excluded from analysis. Furthermore, groups that were moving north were excluded from the analysis. After removing these survey hours and groups, `r signif(sum(new.summary$Complete_Data$dur) * 24, 4)` hours of survey effort with `r sum(daily.counts$n.day)` whales in `r sum(daily.counts$n.group)` groups were retained for further analyses. The estimated total abundance of ENP gray whales during the `r paste0(YEAR-1, "/", YEAR)` southbound migration season was `r estimate.new$Nhat %>% format.big.number()` (95% Credible Interval: `r estimate.new$LCL %>% format.big.number()` - `r estimate.new$UCL %>% format.big.number()`, CV = `r signif(CV.Nhat.new, 3)`, 20-percentile = `r quantile(WinBUGS.results$BUGS.out$sims.list$Corrected.Est[,x], 0.2) %>% format.big.number()`). This estimate includes the multiplicative correction factor for nighttime passage (mean = 1.0875, SD = 0.03625, Perryman et al. 1999). This estimate represents a `r abs(signif((estimate.new$Nhat - estimate.last.year$Nhat)/estimate.last.year$Nhat, 3)) * 100`% increase from the previous estimate for the `r seasons[(length(seasons)-1)]` season (`r estimate.last.year$Nhat %>% format.big.number()`; Table \@ref(tab:Table-Nhats-v1)). After the observed decrease between the 2023/2024 and 2024/2025 seasons, this increase was an encouraging finding (Figure \@ref(fig:Figure-nhats-v1)). 

The observed rates of increase in recent years are biologically implausible. A large whale population cannot increase in abundance by \>10%. Consequently, some factors other than demographic events (i.e., births and deaths) must be affecting the observed numbers of whales along the central California coast. These factors may include sampling error, analytical bias, changes in gray whale migration behavior, and sighting conditions, as well as combinations of these factors. We plan to address these possibilities by designing targeted studies in the near future. 

Using the long time series of estimated abundance of ENP gray whales from Granite Canyon, CA, a recent integrated population modeling analysis indicated that fluctuations in the gray whale abundance was likely associated with availability of benthic prey in the Arctic region ([@stewart_boom-bust_2023]). The time series suggests that observed fluctuations are common. The observed declines in abundance might represent short-term events that had not resulted in any detectable longer-term impacts on the population. That is, despite occasional declines in abundance since the time-series of data began in 1967, the population continues to fluctuate (Figure \@ref(fig:Figure-nhats-v1)). Given the rapid changes in the ocean environment, especially in the Arctic region where gray whales forage, continuous monitoring is essential to determine the extent of future fluctuations. NOAA/NMFS plan to closely monitor the population with regular surveys to estimate abundance, calf production, and body condition of gray whales. The results of these research efforts will continue to provide the best scientific information available on the population's status.

## Acknowledgements {.unnumbered}

We thank our visual observer team for their diligence and meticulous data recording, sometimes in the inclement weather. Annette Henry, Lynn Evans, Tina Chen, and Robin LeRoux provided logistical support and survey planning to successfully carry out the mission. Bob Brownell graciously provided us with space in his office and local hospitality. We thank X and Robert Luckert at the Marine Pollution lab at Granite Canyon who continuously support our field effort at the study site. Their friendship and problem solving onsite are invaluable. X and Y improved this work by way of their careful reviews. Funding for this project was provided by NOAA/NMFS.

## Literature cited {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::

\newpage

## Tables and figures {.unnumbered}

```{r Table-Nhats-v1, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
flextable(all.estimates.new %>% dplyr::select(-c(Year, Nhat.2025))) %>% 
  set_caption(paste0("Estimated abundance (Nhat) and 95% lower (LCL) and upper (UCL) confidence limits of gray whales from the visual surveys off Granite Canyon, CA. Estimates prior to the 2006/2007 season are from Laake et al. (2012), where confidence limits were computed using the lognormal distribution. For the 2006/2007 through 2024/2025 seasons, the method of Durban et al. (2016) was used. "))  %>%
  colformat_double(j = c("LCL", "UCL", "SE"), digits = 1) %>%
  set_table_properties(width = 0.5, layout = "autofit")
```

\newpage

```{r Figure-daily-fit, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, fig.cap="Daily estimated numbers of gray whales migrating through the sampling area off Granite Canyon. Black lines are medians and orange bands indicate 95% credible intervals from the method of Durban et al (2015). Solid circles indicate observed counts adjusted for the daily survey effort."}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("Figures/daily_estimates_", YEAR, ".png"))

```

\newpage

```{r Figure-nhats-v1, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, fig.cap="Estimated abundance and 95% CIs (confidence intervals for the method of Laake et al. and credible intervals for the method of Durban et al.) of gray whales from the visual surveys off Granite Canyon, CA, between the 1967/1968 and 2024/2025 seasons. Estimates in green indicate those from Laake et al. (2012) whereas those in red (from the 2006/2007 season) indicate using the method in Durban et al. (2016). Yellow bars represent unusual mortality events."}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("Figures/nhats_", YEAR, ".png"))

```
